value: The old bookshop on Elm Street had survived three recessions, two floods, and the arrival of Amazon. Margaret had tried to retire twice. She suspected it had something to do with the cat. Her daughter thought she was projecting. Maybe she was. Both times, Whiskers had knocked her "CLOSING SALE" sign off the door. Whiskers — a enormous orange tabby of indeterminate age — had claimed the front window as his throne sometime around 2003, and customers kept coming back just to see him. "I only came in because of the cat," they'd say, then leave with four novels and a cookbook. She took it as a sign — the metaphorical kind, not the cardboard one lying on the floor. Margaret Chen didn't know how, exactly. But the shop was still open, the cat was still fat, and the books still smelled like promises.
